plan.contextChunk:
One classical example of the limitations of motion estimation from
images is the **aperture problem**. The aperture problem happens when
observing the motion of a one-dimensional (1D) structure larger than the
image frame, as shown in @fig-apperture_problem. If none of the
termination points are in view within the observation window (i.e., the
aperture), it is impossible to measure the actual image motion. Only the
component orthogonal to the moving structure can be measured.

![Aperture problem when observing the motion of a one-dimensional (1D) structure larger than the image frame. The actual motion of the bar is upward, but the perception, when vision is limited to what is visible within the observation window, appears as if the motion of the bar is in the direction perpendicular to the bar.](figures/optical_flow/apperture_problem.png){width="100%" #fig-apperture_problem}


:::{.column-margin}
The **Barber-pole illusion** is an illustration of the aperture problem. The barber pole induces the illusion of downward motion while rotating.

![](figures/optical_flow/barber_pole.png){width="40%"}
:::


### Representation of Optical Flow

We can model motion over time as a sequence of **dense optical flow**
images: $$\left[ u(x,y,t), v(x,y,t) \right]$$ The quantities $u(x,y,t)$and $v(x,y,t)$ indicate that a pixel at image coordinates $(x,y)$ at
time $t$ moves with velocity $(u,v)$. The problem with this formulation
is that we do not have an explicit representation of the trajectory
followed by points in the scene over time. As time $t$ changes, the
location $(x,y)$ might correspond to different scene elements.

An alternative representation is to model motion as a **sparse set of
moving points** (tracks):
$$\left\{ \mathbf{P}^{(i)}_t \right\}_{i=1}^N$$ This has the challenge

elements: red, white, and blue diagonal stripes on the cylinder
real rotation motion arrows above cylinder
observed downward motion arrows beside cylinder

interactions.teaches: Allows viewing the pole from various angles to understand how perceived motion changes with viewpoint.

demo_steps.title: Introduction to the Barber Pole
demo_steps.narration: Here is a classic barber pole. Notice the stripes spiraling around the pole. As the pole rotates, observe how the stripes seem to move downwards, illustrating the aperture problem.

demo_steps.title: Rotation and Perception
demo_steps.narration: Rotate the pole and focus on a single stripe. Although it's rotating around, the stripe appears to move linearly downwards due to the limited view, mimicking the illusion.

demo_steps.title: Observing from Different Angles
demo_steps.narration: Change your viewpoint to see how the perception of motion varies. Notice that from different angles, you get a clearer picture of the actual rotation versus the perceived downward motion.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Initial view from a frontal angle showing the cylinder from the side, with a slight tilt to capture the stripes' pattern clearly.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match the original image silhouette, size, label scale, and crop
inline_constraints.visible_ui: none initially; interactions revealed through direct manipulation
inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips and click popups only
inline_constraints.framing: match the source crop and whitespace
inline_constraints.camera: infer the source camera angle/projection/zoom for correct initial appearance

interactionPlan.notes: Use subtle shading to emphasize cylindrical shape; the rotation angle should create the illusion of downward movement in the viewer's frame of reference.

discrepancies: The 'Real motion' arrow in the screenshot is at the wrong angle compared to the source image.
The 'Observed motion' arrow in the screenshot is not correctly positioned beneath the cylinder.
The stripes on the cylinder are not as neatly aligned and diagonal as in the source image.
Missing the top arc arrow representation for 'Real motion'.
The text labels are slightly misaligned compared to the source image.
evaluation.notes: The rendering has alignment and positioning issues with arrows and labels, diverging from the original design.
action_items: Reposition and correct the angle of the 'Real motion' arrow to match the source image's top arc representation.
Adjust the 'Observed motion' arrow to accurately point beneath the cylinder.
Improve the diagonal stripe alignment on the cylinder for clearer visual correspondence.
Ensure text labels are positioned to match the source image's placements.

feedback.rationale: The discrepancies are mainly about the alignment and positioning of elements and labels, which indicates the generator needs refinement rather than an issue with the conceptual plan.
